sections.title: Structure of the Rib Cage
points: The rib cage is made up of 24 individual ribs (12 pairs).
Attachment: At the back, all 12 pairs are attached to the Thoracic vertebrae.
Sternum: Also called the breastbone. It is the flat bone that anchors the ribs at the front.
Costal Cartilage: The flexible tissue that connects ribs to the sternum, allowing the chest to expand.
Intercostal Muscles: Found between the ribs; they pull the ribs during breathing.

sections.title: Three Types of Ribs
points: 1. True Ribs (Pairs 1-7): They attach directly to the sternum using their own cartilage.
2. False Ribs (Pairs 8-10): They attach to the cartilage of the rib above them, not the sternum.
3. Floating Ribs (Pairs 11-12): They only attach to the spine and 'float' at the front.

sections.title: Breathing Mechanics (The Pump)
points: Inhalation (Breathing In): Ribs move up and out + Diaphragm flattens. Volume increases, pressure drops, air rushes in.
Exhalation (Breathing Out): Ribs move down and in + Diaphragm domes. Volume decreases, pressure rises, air is forced out.

sections.text: The rib cage is flexible because of cartilage. This allows for Respiration. If the cage were solid bone, the lungs could not expand, and the person would suffocate!

sections.title: Key Functions
points: Protection: Shields the Heart and Lungs. The lower ribs also protect the Liver and Spleen.
Movement: Provides the framework for the Intercostal muscles to act as a bellows.
Blood Factory: The flat bones (ribs/sternum) contain Red Bone Marrow to make blood cells.

cards.fact: The first 7 pairs that touch the breastbone.
The last 2 pairs (11 & 12) with no front attachment.
Big volume = Low pressure (In); Small volume = High pressure (Out).
cards.mnemonic: Think of a Squeeze—it pushes the air Out!
